html:
III

‚ÄúYou say he‚Äôs different,‚Äù pursued Joyce interestedly. ‚ÄúHow do you mean‚Å†‚Äîdifferent? Sober, perhaps?‚Äù

Masterson chuckled.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt be a fool!‚Äù she growled. ‚ÄúYou know very well what I meant.‚Äù

He returned his empty glass to the silver tray on the table, settled himself comfortably into the cushions of the garden swing, and so frankly considered the slender shapeliness of the girl in the wicker chair that she shifted her position uneasily.

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù he replied, reverting tardily to her question, ‚Äúhe‚Äôs all of sober, and then some. He‚Äôs owlish‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ morose‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ prowls the night like Hamlet‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ has an idea that people resent his having been saved from drowning.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow absurd! Did he tell you that?‚Äù

‚ÄúAs much as.‚Äù

She thumbed the pages of the novel that lay in her lap and frowned.

‚ÄúWell‚Å†‚Äîand what is he proposing to do about it?‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Sulk?‚Äù

Young Masterson indicated by a slow shake of the head, eyes half closed, that the problem was too vast for him, and meditatively tapped the end of a fresh cigarette on the arm of the swing.

‚ÄúYou‚Äôll discover for yourself that Bobby is greatly altered since his accident. I can‚Äôt quite make him out. Yesterday, when I saw him at Windymere, I expected to find him in better spirits. He is almost well now; has been walking about on the grounds for days. But he seems thoroughly preoccupied. I suggested it might improve his disposition if we threw together a little cocktail, and he said, ‚ÄòYou know where the makings are: help yourself.‚Äô I shook up enough for both of us, but he wouldn‚Äôt join me; and when I ragged him about it, he replied, from about ten miles off, that he‚Äôd ‚Äòanother plan in mind.‚Äô

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòSomething that doesn‚Äôt include gin, evidently,‚Äô I suggested; and he nodded cryptically.

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòSomething like that,‚Äô he replied. You know about how little he discloses through that poker face of his, when he decides to be incomunicado.‚Äù

‚ÄúSo‚Å†‚Äîyou dared him to tell you, I suppose.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo; I just kidded him a little, but he didn‚Äôt take it very nicely. Just sat‚Å†‚Äîand posed for ‚ÄòThe Thinker.‚Äô ‚ÄòWhat‚Äôs the big idea?‚Äô I said. ‚ÄòGone over to Andy Volstead?‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat did he say?‚Äù demanded Joyce, as the pause lengthened.

‚ÄúHe said, ‚ÄòHell, no!‚Äô and then mumbled, down in his throat, that he‚Äôd gone over to Nancy Ashford.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd who‚Äôs Nancy Ashford?‚Äù she inquired, sharply, flushing with annoyance over her disclosure.

‚ÄúYou ought to know,‚Äù smugly enjoying her vexation. ‚ÄúShe is the superintendent of Brightwood Hospital.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh‚Å†‚Äîyou mean Mrs. Ashford. I hadn‚Äôt thought of her as Nancy. They must have become quite well acquainted. Why, she‚Äôs an old lady.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell‚Å†‚Äîso much the better; wouldn‚Äôt you say?‚Äù

She met his banter with a grimace.

‚ÄúYou spend too much of your time thinking up story plots, Tommy. It‚Äôs affecting your mind.‚Äù

‚ÄúMaybe so,‚Äù agreed Masterson dryly. He stretched his long arms over the back of the swing and regarded her with an inquisitive smile. ‚ÄúYour own story grows more exciting every minute. What else do you want to know about Bobby?‚Äù

Joyce offered him the concession of a crooked smile.

‚ÄúDid he say whether he was coming in soon?‚Äù

‚ÄúNary a word on that. However, he may not feel himself quite up to it yet‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Rather awkward situation, you‚Äôll admit.‚Äù

She nodded, and there was a moment‚Äôs silence.

‚ÄúYou have Bobby and me all wrong, Tom. We were together pretty steadily‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ in December‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ before Helen came‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶‚Äù

Masterson broke in with an unpleasant chuckle.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm surprised that you remember anything about December,‚Äù he teased. ‚ÄúMy own recollection of it is very pale.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes; I‚Äôll admit it was rather dreadful. Especially the evening we celebrated your birthday. That must have been a mighty rough night on the sea. Incidentally, I have not seen Bobby since. When he finished at the university in February, he sailed for France to visit his mother, without a line to me that he was going. I had two short letters from him later. Then he turns up at home; next day this dreadful thing happens to us.‚Äù

She hesitated before going on.

‚ÄúSo‚Å†‚Äînow you know exactly how thick we are. Does it sound‚Å†‚Äîromantic like?‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course, you‚Äôre bound to keep it in mind,‚Äù observed Masterson soberly, ‚Äúthat Bobby feels quite terribly about the‚Å†‚Äîthe thing that occurred out there at the lake. Never having met Helen, he is a bit shy about meeting her now. He may fear she would be slightly prejudiced against him, under the circumstances.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm afraid she is,‚Äù agreed Joyce reluctantly. ‚ÄúEntirely natural that she would be.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow is Helen, by the way?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, she‚Äôs steady‚Å†‚Äîthe darling! Want to see her? I‚Äôll tell her you‚Äôre here.‚Äù

She rose, handing Masterson her book.

‚ÄúHelen has been entertaining a queer little lady for the past hour or more, but I think she is free now. The caller was one of father‚Äôs patients, I presume. So many people have been here lately‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ all sorts‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ people we had never heard of who come with tearful gratitude to tell us what father had been to them. Really, it has kept us quite stirred up. I wish they wouldn‚Äôt‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ And letters?‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Today there was a long one from a man in Maine hinting that father had saved his life, somehow, years ago. He didn‚Äôt state the particulars‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Seemed rather secretive, as if there were some big mystery behind it; as if there were something he wanted to tell, but couldn‚Äôt. Very queer‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ I‚Äôll go and call Helen.‚Äù

She turned toward the big white house with the green shutters, and Masterson‚Äôs eyes appraisingly followed her graceful movements as she crossed the lawn‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Some girl!‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ He set the swing gently in motion and inhaled deeply from his cigarette‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ A thoroughbred! So, she was Bobby‚Äôs, then? What the devil did Bobby mean‚Å†‚Äîtrying to keep this a secret from him? Well, if she considered herself Bobby‚Äôs property‚Å†‚Äîand obviously she did‚Å†‚ÄîBobby‚Äôs pal must be loyal. However, a man could look at her, couldn‚Äôt he?‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ And wish she belonged to him?‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ A compliment, in a way‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ perhaps‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ debatable question, probably‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ But seriously, why shouldn‚Äôt an artist in creative writing have as much licence to admire beauty for its own lovely sake as a painter‚Å†‚Äîno matter whose girl she thought she was? What a type! Not many blondes like that left in this dyed and painted world‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Original colours, these‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Pale gold and milk white; with the slinky-footed gait of some wild woods thing‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Some girl! But what made her think Bobby was interested in her? Or‚Å†‚Äîwas he? If so, he had kept his sentiments carefully concealed‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶

Joyce‚Äôs reappearance through the shrubbery, accompanied by her stepmother, interrupted Masterson‚Äôs daydreaming. The two offered a striking contrast. Mrs.¬†Hudson was Latin in every feature and curve, in the glossy blackness of her shingled hair, the arch of her brows, the utter lack of self-consciousness in her posture and carriage. Joyce was perfect Saxon; slightly the taller. Leading the way, she seemed the older.

He strolled to meet them. Helen waved her hand, upon sight of him. She had adopted the role of being years his senior, much to his amusement. They had often made a little game of it‚Å†‚Äîhe, cast for the part of a spoiled nine-year-old, which he carried off with amazing skill; she, the exasperated but polite mother, endeavouring to keep her whelp in hand without making too much of a scene. They had done it, quite spontaneously, at the Byrnes, one evening. Laura Byrne, upon her own testimony, ‚Äúhad about passed out,‚Äù Senator Byrne had said the skit was worth a fortune in vaudeville ‚Äúbig time.‚Äù They had thought it not half bad, themselves.

The conventional uniform of the bereft had only accentuated her youth. It called attention to her girlish vitality, deepened her dimples, whitened her throat; as a severe frame on a bright etching will heighten its colours; emphasize its values.

She extended a small hand and smiled. Her recent experience had left traces. She was pale and a bit remote, her appearance suggesting convalescence from a serious illness.

The smile fluttered momentarily and was gone; but it was essentially the same smile that one waited for, plotted for, tried to recapture in memory, analyzed without success. Once Masterson had attempted to persuade one of his women in a story to smile like that; but she couldn‚Äôt learn it. He had written, of ‚ÄúGloria‚Äù‚Å†‚Äî

‚ÄúIt was more than a smile. It was a little sonata, in three movements. It arrived first in her eyes, which gradually grew wider and bluer. Almost imperceptibly, but very disturbingly, the patrician brows lifted, ever so little, as if they asked permission. That was the adagio movement.

‚ÄúThen, suddenly, the sonata played upon her lips, as when an organist seeks with one hand a lower bank of keys for the melody. They parted to disclose the smallest, straightest, whitest of teeth. That was the scherzo movement.

‚ÄúInstantly, however, as if the lips had become alarmed at their own audacity, they closed, demurely. But the smile lingered in her eyes‚Å†‚Äîin the outer corners of her eyes‚Å†‚Äîlong after her pretty mouth had done with it; and that was the largo movement. Largo dolcemente.

‚ÄúAnd the beholder? What of him? Ah‚Å†‚Äîbut his pulse ran on into throbbing, pounding strotto!‚Äù

Masterson knew the description was silly. He had added, rather helplessly:

‚Äú‚Å†‚Äîa vastly disquieting smile; a smile to be smiled with discretion, preferably among strong-minded, elderly men‚Å†‚Äîrelatives, if at all convenient to have relatives at hand.‚Äù

Helen Hudson smiled. Today it was a sonatina. The movements were adagio, andante, lento; but it was no less stirring for its chastened mood.

‚ÄúIt seems a long time since we saw you, Tom,‚Äù she said, in her husky contralto, motioning him to a place beside her in the swing.

Joyce had remained standing.

‚ÄúTommy,‚Äù said she, ‚ÄúI had promised Ned Brownlow I would go for a ride with him. He‚Äôs out front now, waiting for me‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Mind if I go?‚Äù

‚ÄúDepart in Peace!‚Äù Masterson held up two fingers, pontifically. ‚ÄúI am in excellent hands.‚Äù

Joyce‚Äôs slim fingers trailed caressingly across her stepmother‚Äôs shoulders as she moved away. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll not be long, dear,‚Äù she said.

‚ÄúHave you been getting out, at all?‚Äù inquired Masterson, with comradely solicitude.

Helen shook her head.

‚ÄúToo busy! Callers come here at all hours; people one can‚Äôt very well refuse to see; patients of Doctor Hudson‚Äôs and others he appears to have befriended in one way or another. I presume you recall the quite unusual number of floral remembrances‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúI never saw so many!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, Tom, those flowers came at the direction of people from many places; from persons whose relation to us was very difficult to establish. Fully a score were unidentifiable by anyone at Brightwood. And these callers I am receiving daily are mostly unknown to us. They come to inquire if there is anything they can do for Joyce and me. Yesterday a queer old Italian turned up and tried to present me with a thousand dollars. That‚Äôs just a sample. Their stories are quite different, what there is of them‚Å†‚Äîfor they are strangely reticent‚Å†‚Äîbut one fact is common to all‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ sometime, somewhere, Doctor Hudson had helped them meet a crisis‚Å†‚Äîusually involving money loaned; though not always money; sometimes just advice, and the aid of his influence.‚Äù

‚ÄúHe surely had a big heart!‚Äù said Masterson.

‚ÄúYes, certainly; but there‚Äôs more to it than that. Lots of men have big hearts, and are generous with their money. This is a different matter. His dealings with these people were something other. They all act as if they belonged with him to some eccentric secret society. They come here eager to do something, anything, for me, because they want to express their gratitude; but when you pin them down and invite them to tell you by what process they got into our family‚Äôs debt, they stammer and dodge. It‚Äôs very strange.

‚ÄúSince two o‚Äôclock, I‚Äôve been listening to a story that perplexes me more than any of the others; probably because I prodded a little deeper into the mystery. An old lady I didn‚Äôt know existed came to tell me what a wonderful man Doctor Hudson was, and could she be of any aid to me?‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ I‚Äôd like to talk it over with someone. Would it bore you?‚Äù

‚ÄúTell me‚Å†‚Äîplease!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell‚Å†‚Äîit all began, said Mrs.¬†Wickes, with an operation on her husband. Doctor Hudson had warned them it was hopeless. The family was left destitute. She says there was no bill sent from the hospital; that Doctor Hudson found a good position for the older boy and sent the girl, who had some talent for drawing, to an art school‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ She pointed to that lovely marine over the mantel in our living-room, and said, ‚ÄòThat‚Äôs hers. She gave it to him. It was exhibited by the Architectural League in New York.‚Äô‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Doctor Hudson had stood between them and disaster, she said, until they were able to look out for themselves; and when, a couple years later, she went to his office with a small payment on the debt they owed him, he refused to accept it; said, at first, that he had had enough joy out of it, and didn‚Äôt want to be repaid any farther. She quite insisted; and he said, ‚ÄòDid you ever tell anybody about our little transaction?‚Äô ‚ÄòNo,‚Äô she replied. ‚ÄòYou told me not to‚Å†‚Äîand I didn‚Äôt.‚Äô ‚ÄòThen,‚Äô he declared stoutly, ‚ÄòI can‚Äôt take it back!‚Äô

‚ÄúOf course, she was dissatisfied to leave it that way‚Å†‚Äîshe has all the instincts of a lady‚Å†‚Äîbut when she pressed him to take the money he explained, ‚ÄòHad I considered this a loan, I could accept its repayment. I did not so regard it, when I invested it in your family. You have all been much more successful and prosperous than I thought you were going to be. So‚Å†‚Äîsince I believed I was giving it to you outright, I can‚Äôt take it back now because, in the meantime, I have used it all up, myself!‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúPardon me,‚Äù said Masterson, ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt believe I quite understand. What was that last thing he said to her?‚Äù

Helen nodded, mysteriously, and repeated the inexplicable phrase.

‚ÄúYou may well inquire,‚Äù she went on. ‚ÄúI asked Mrs.¬†Wickes what that meant, and she grew restless. ‚ÄòI can‚Äôt say that I rightly know,‚Äô she stammered.

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòBut you have a suspicion!‚Äô I said.

‚ÄúAt that she hurriedly changed the subject by taking a bulky purse from her handbag. She pressed me to take the money. It had been invested, she said, and now she wished to restore it to us.

‚ÄúI said to her, ‚ÄòIf Doctor Hudson refused to accept it, so shall I. You had best reinvest it. Put it back where it was, if it yielded a good rate.‚Äô

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòOh, I can‚Äôt do that,‚Äô she replied. ‚ÄòThey‚Äôre quite done with it, you know.‚Äô

‚ÄúWell, after that, I gave it up! She was over my head!‚Äù

‚ÄúPerhaps she‚Äôs a bit cracked,‚Äù hazarded Masterson.

Helen was thoughtful.

‚ÄúYes; she would be cracked, and we could let it go at that, and smile over it, if she was the only one of her species.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou mean that you‚Äôve entertained some more like her?‚Äù

She nodded.

‚ÄúYesterday, a quite well-known merchant called on me. You would recognize his name. His affair with Doctor Hudson dated ten years back. He wanted to pay me a pretty large sum of money which he said was interest on a loan. I thought it odd that he was coming forward with it after so long a time, and he confessed that Doctor Hudson had refused it. So did I, of course.

‚ÄúMy curiosity had the better of me, and I pressed him to tell me about it. He said that about ten years ago he was on the rim of failure. He had started in business for himself; had overreached; and, as if he was not having enough anxiety, his wife passed through a lengthy and expensive illness. He had built a beautiful home. It was more than half paid for. He decided to let it go at a cruel sacrifice to get cash to put into his tottering business. He listed the house with a real estate concern. It was worth thirty-five thousand dollars. He was offering it for twenty thousand. There was a temporary depression of real estate values. Well‚Å†‚Äînext day, he said, Doctor Hudson came to see him.

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòI understand you‚Äôre selling your home for twenty thousand dollars. Why are you doing that? It‚Äôs worth twice that much.‚Äô

‚ÄúThe young merchant explained that he must have money immediately, or his business would fail.

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòI‚Äôll lend you twenty thousand,‚Äô said Doctor Hudson. ‚ÄòI haven‚Äôt it, but I can get it. Pay me the principal of this loan when you are prospering again. I shall not expect any interest, because I have use for it, myself; and you are not to tell anyone, while I live, that we have transacted this business.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat an odd deal,‚Äù commented Masterson.

‚ÄúWait until you hear the rest of it,‚Äù said Helen quietly. ‚ÄúWithin three years, my caller said, he had returned the money, and was insistent upon paying interest on the loan. Doctor Hudson refused to take it. And what do you think he said when he declined to accept the money?‚Äù

‚ÄúGive up!‚Äù

‚ÄúHe said, ‚ÄòI can‚Äôt take it, you see; for I‚Äôve used it all up myself!‚Äô Now‚Å†‚Äîthat‚Äôs five distinct times I‚Äôve heard that phrase, in the past week! What do you make of it?‚Äù

‚ÄúQueer!‚Äù said Masterson. ‚ÄúCouldn‚Äôt have had something to do with his income tax, could it? You know‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ so much allowed for gifts, charity, and the like.‚Äù

‚ÄúTommy, don‚Äôt be foolish!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell‚Å†‚Äîhave you a theory?‚Äù

‚ÄúNot the faintest glimmer of one.‚Äù Then, animatedly, ‚ÄúDid you ever hear the story of Doctor Hudson‚Äôs early life?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo; does it offer a clue to these queer performances of his?‚Äù

‚ÄúNot at all‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ at least, it offers no clue to me. Perhaps‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ to a psychologist, which I am not‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ But, I think I‚Äôd like to tell you‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ It‚Äôs no secret.

‚ÄúYou see, Wayne‚Äôs parents were very poor. They lived on a farm upstate somewhere. He had to look out for himself, early. As a boy he had wanted to be a surgeon. He came to Detroit, at fifteen, to enter high school, and worked in the home of a Doctor Cummings‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd married his daughter! I knew that much.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou‚Äôre going too fast‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ In the Cummings home, Wayne Hudson was errand boy, hostler, accountant, and, on occasion, nurse, cook, private secretary, and rescue squad.‚Äù

‚ÄúRescue? How‚Äôs that?‚Äù

Helen hesitated.

‚ÄúThis Doctor Cummings was a very capable man, with a large practice; but unfortunately he drank too much‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ periodically. At intervals of anywhere from three weeks to two months, he would disappear for days. It was Wayne‚Äôs duty to track him down, clean him up, bring him home, and meantime invent excuses for his absence and serve as a shock-absorber between the doctor and all his interests‚Å†‚Äîthe hospitals, the patients, the family.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot a very pleasant occupation for a high school boy.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù agreed Helen, ‚Äúbut calculated to mature him early. And it was not by any means a thankless task. Doctor Cummings was of course deeply appreciative and in his repentant moments assured him of his lasting gratitude. He sent Wayne to college later, and guaranteed his medical training with a life insurance policy, which, strangely enough, became accessible exactly when he had most need of it, for Doctor Cummings died when Wayne was a senior in college.‚Äù

‚ÄúPerhaps that helps to explain Doctor Hudson‚Äôs marriage while he was still a medic,‚Äù commented Masterson. ‚ÄúDoubtless the girl was fond of him. He felt under heavy obligations to the family. That‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ and the propinquity‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ so he married her.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot quite that,‚Äù corrected Helen. ‚ÄúHe was very fond of her; had given up everything to be in Arizona with her until she died. For more than four years, she was his chief concern. Naturally, he couldn‚Äôt give proper attention to his work. He told me he had days of depression, while in the medical school, fearing he had mistaken his vocation, after all. His studies were hard, and he had much difficulty keeping up with them.‚Äù

‚ÄúOne would hardly think that Doctor Hudson had ever found his studies difficult.‚Äù

‚ÄúHe continued to find them so, for fully a year after his wife‚Äôs death. Then, something happened! No; I do not know what it was. He did not tell me, and I did not insist; but something happened! One day he became conscious of a new attitude towards his books, his profession. He worked whole nights in the hospital laboratory, without fatigue. Then, soon after, through an odd circumstance, he was obliged to do a difficult operation at three o‚Äôclock one morning on an emergency case‚Å†‚Äîa head injury. It attracted much attention. From then on he specialized in brain surgery. You know how well he succeeded.‚Äù

Masterson closed one eye, and considered her thoughtfully.

‚ÄúI can see,‚Äù he said, measuring his words, ‚Äúthat it‚Äôs somehow in the back of your head that this rather remarkable change in him‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ this quite sudden step-up from depression‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ sense of failure‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ halfway notion to quit medicine and sell bonds or something‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ into prompt recognition and success‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ I think you suspect that it‚Äôs all tied around this‚Å†‚Äîthis funny business of his charities? Am I right?‚Äù

She nodded.

‚ÄúMostly however because here are two mysteries about him. I suppose I have tried to relate them‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ unconsciously, perhaps. They may have no association, at all‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Maybe he would have told me all about it, had he lived‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ But‚Å†‚Äîwe‚Äôve talked enough about mysteries, Tommy. Let‚Äôs go and look at the asters.‚Äù

Masterson followed her through the garden, admiring her childish enthusiasm over the autumn flowers. It was as if she caressed them. He knew she expected him to go now, and toyed with his keys.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt stay in too closely,‚Äù he admonished. ‚ÄúThese people will wear you out.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm taking a few days off‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ going up into the country tomorrow, to see Martha, our caretaker‚Äôs sister. She‚Äôs not very well‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ dreadfully broken up, you know; and I haven‚Äôt seen her since it all happened.‚Äù

‚ÄúMight I drive you up? I should like to!‚Äù

‚ÄúThanks; but I shall want my car while I am there.‚Äù

‚ÄúWe might tow it!‚Äù

‚ÄúOh‚Å†‚Äîdo you really want to go up there so badly as that? I‚Äôll tell you what you may do; drive Joyce up to Flintridge, Sunday afternoon. Probably I‚Äôll be lonesome by that time.‚Äù

They strolled together to the gate.

‚ÄúHow fine it is that you and Joyce still have each other!‚Äù

‚ÄúYes; isn‚Äôt it?‚Äù

He stepped into his car, waved a hand, and disappeared around the corner. Slowly Helen retraced her steps to the garden, sauntered along the narrow path, stooped to cup her pink palms around a garish dahlia. How fine it was that she and Joyce still had each other‚Å†‚Ää‚Å†‚Ä¶ Or had they?
